records.ety: [OE. stumblen, stomblen; freq. of a word akin to E. stammer. See Stammer.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Stumbled (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Stumbling (?).]

defs: To trip in walking or in moving in any way with the legs; to strike the foot so as to fall, or to endanger a fall; to stagger because of a false step.
quotes: There stumble steeds strong and down go all.
The way of the wicked is as darkness: they know at what they stumble.

defs: To walk in an unsteady or clumsy manner.
quotes: He stumbled up the dark avenue.

defs: To fall into a crime or an error; to err.
quotes: He that loveth his brother abideth in the light, and there is none occasion og stumbling in him.

defs: To strike or happen (upon a person or thing) without design; to fall or light by chance; -- with on, upon, or against.
quotes: Ovid stumbled, by some inadvertency, upon Livia in a bath.
Forth as she waddled in the brake, A gray goose stumbled on a snake.

defs: To cause to stumble or trip.
Fig.: To mislead; to confound; to perplex; to cause to err or to fall.
quotes: False and dazzling fires to stumble men.
One thing more stumbles me in the very foundation of this hypothesis.

defs: A trip in walking or running.
A blunder; a failure; a fall from rectitude.
quotes: One stumble is enough to deface the character of an honorable life.
